name: Titanium Carbide Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Titanium Carbide. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Titanium Carbide surface cleaning without substrate damage

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength optimized for Titanium Carbide absorption and minimal substrate interaction

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning of Titanium Carbide surfaces

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for continuous cleaning coverage with thermal management

fluenceThreshold.description: Minimum fluence required for effective Titanium Carbide contaminant removal

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for efficient ablation of Titanium Carbide contaminants

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for efficient cleaning coverage

passCount.description: Recommended number of passes for complete contaminant removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning without excessive thermal buildup

power.description: Typical range for industrial cleaning of hard ceramics like TiC to balance ablation and avoid thermal damage; starting at 100 W for light contamination. Max limited by substrate melting point (~3067°C). Source: IPG Photonics YLR series specs for 1064 nm cleaning heads; academic review in 'Laser Cleaning of Metals' (Optics & Lasers in Engineering, 2018).

scan_speed.description: Effective speeds for contamination removal on reflective/high-conductivity materials like TiC; higher for light contamination (up to 5000 mm/s), lower for heavy (500-1000 mm/s). Optimal balances fluence and coverage. Source: Industry standards from Laserax equipment manuals; case studies in Journal of Laser Applications (2020).

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective cleaning of TiC with minimal HAZ due to its high thermal conductivity (~20-30 W/m·K); picosecond (ps) or femtosecond for ultra-precise but less common industrially. Avoids excessive heat accumulation. Source: Laser physics in 'Pulsed Laser Ablation of Ceramics' (Applied Surface Science, 2019).

repetition_rate.description: Typical for 1064 nm cleaning to achieve uniform energy delivery on TiC; 50 kHz balances speed and heat management—higher rates risk accumulation on low-reflectivity TiC (~0.3-0.5 at 1064 nm). Trade-off: faster processing vs. potential overheating. Source: Academic literature in Optics Express (2017) on carbide ablation.

energy_density.description: Fluence range for contaminant removal without damaging TiC; damage threshold ~4-5 J/cm² for ns pulses due to high melting point. Recommended conservative for safe cleaning of oxides or oils. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Journal of Applied Physics (2021) for TiC at 1064 nm.

spot_size.description: Beam diameter for precise surface prep on TiC; smaller (50-100 μm) for fine details, larger (200-500 μm) for broad areas. 200 μm optimal for uniform cleaning without excessive overlap heating. Source: Equipment specs from Coherent and Trumpf laser systems for industrial cleaning.

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-3) for heavy layers on TiC to ensure complete removal without substrate erosion. Recommended 2 passes for industrial prep. Source: Industry case studies in Surface and Coatings Technology (2019) for carbide tools.

overlap_ratio.description: Ensures uniform coverage on TiC surfaces; 60% typical for even fluence distribution and minimal striping, adjustable 30% for speed vs. 80% for thoroughness. Source: Process optimization in Laser & Photonics Reviews (2020).
